name: Moong Dal Dhokla
instructions: Blend soaked moong dal with ginger, chilli and enough water to make a thick batter.
Mix in lemon juice and salt, then fold in fruit salt immediately before steaming.
Steam in a greased tray for 15 minutes or until a skewer comes out clean.
Temper mustard seeds and curry leaves in one teaspoon oil, spread over the dhokla and cut.
substitutions: Use red chilli powder instead of green chilli.
Use baking soda plus extra lemon if fruit salt is unavailable.
tips: Soaking time is additional.
Do not overmix after adding fruit salt.

name: Oats Vegetable Handvo
instructions: Mix oat flour, vegetables, peanut curd, ginger-chilli paste, salt and enough water for a thick batter.
Rest for 10 minutes and fold in fruit salt.
Pour into a greased pan, scatter sesame seeds and cook covered on low heat for 12 minutes.
Flip carefully and cook until both sides are browned and the centre is set.
substitutions: Use soy curd if peanuts are suitable to avoid.
Use grated courgette if bottle gourd is unavailable.
tips: Squeeze only excess water from the bottle gourd; some moisture keeps handvo tender.

name: Bajra Methi Thepla
instructions: Combine both flours, fenugreek, ginger, turmeric and salt.
Add warm water gradually and knead into a soft dough; rest for 10 minutes.
Pat portions between parchment sheets into thin rounds.
Cook each thepla on a hot tawa with a few drops of oil until brown spots appear on both sides.
substitutions: Use dried fenugreek leaves, reducing the amount to 3 tablespoons.
tips: Bajra has no gluten, so patting is easier than rolling.

name: Chole Palak
instructions: Wilt spinach in a covered pan, cool slightly and blend coarsely.
Sauté onion in oil, add ginger-garlic paste, tomato and chole masala, and cook until thick.
Add chickpeas, spinach and half a cup of water.
Simmer for 12 minutes and finish with lemon juice.
substitutions: Use cooked kala chana instead of chickpeas.
tips: Pair with one roti or a small rice portion for a balanced meal.

name: Paneer Tikka Millet Bowl
instructions: Coat paneer and capsicum with curd, tikka masala and salt; rest for 15 minutes.
Grill or air-fry at 200°C until the edges colour, turning once.
Place warm millet and cucumber in bowls.
Top with paneer tikka and a squeeze of lemon.
substitutions: Use firm tofu and unsweetened plant curd for a vegan version.
tips: Do not overcrowd the grill tray or the paneer will steam.

name: Rajma Brown Rice Bowl
instructions: Sauté onion until golden, then cook ginger-garlic paste for one minute.
Add tomato puree and rajma masala and cook until the mixture thickens.
Add kidney beans and one cup water; simmer for 15 minutes, lightly mashing a few beans.
Serve over measured portions of brown rice.
substitutions: Use white rice for a softer texture, noting the lower fiber.
tips: Boil dried kidney beans thoroughly; undercooked kidney beans are unsafe.

name: Gatte Ki Sabzi
instructions: Knead chickpea flour with spices, a teaspoon of oil and enough water into a firm dough.
Shape into logs, boil for 10 minutes, cool and slice into gatte.
Whisk curd with water and spices.
Cook the curd gravy gently while stirring, add gatte and simmer for 8 minutes.
substitutions: Use unsweetened soy curd for a dairy-free version.
tips: Lower the heat before adding curd to reduce splitting.

name: Ker Sangri
instructions: Soak ker and sangri overnight, rinse repeatedly and boil until tender.
Heat mustard oil and bloom fennel seeds.
Add the drained vegetables and dry spices.
Cook uncovered for 10 minutes, finishing with amchur.
substitutions: Use a neutral oil if mustard is unsuitable.
tips: Multiple rinses reduce the strong salty-tart flavour of dried ker.

name: Pesarattu with Ginger Chutney
instructions: Blend soaked moong, rice, half the ginger, chilli, cumin, salt and water into a pourable batter.
Spread a ladleful thinly on a hot tawa and scatter a little onion.
Cook with a few drops of oil until crisp, flip briefly and remove.
Blend remaining ginger with lemon, chilli and a little jaggery for a quick chutney.
substitutions: Omit rice for an entirely pulse-based batter.
tips: Soaking time is additional; the batter does not need fermentation.

name: Millet Sambar Vegetable Bowl
instructions: Simmer vegetables with tamarind, turmeric and sambar powder until tender.
Add cooked toor dal and enough water for a pourable consistency.
Simmer for 10 minutes and season with salt.
Temper mustard seeds and curry leaves, add to sambar and serve with millet.
substitutions: Use brown rice or quinoa instead of little millet.
tips: Choose at least three vegetables for better texture and variety.

name: Lemon Kodo Millet
instructions: Cool cooked millet so the grains remain separate.
Heat oil, roast peanuts, then add mustard seeds and curry leaves.
Add turmeric and the millet, tossing gently with salt.
Turn off the heat and mix in lemon juice.
substitutions: Use roasted chana dal instead of peanuts if appropriate.
tips: Add lemon only after turning off the heat to keep the flavour bright.

name: Kerala Vegetable Stew with Appam
instructions: Cook the vegetables with ginger, chilli, pepper, salt and a little water until just tender.
Add light coconut milk and warm gently without a hard boil.
Adjust seasoning and finish with curry leaves.
Serve one bowl of stew with two small appams per person.
substitutions: Serve with idiyappam or red rice instead of appam.
tips: Use light coconut milk to keep the dish creamy without making it too heavy.

name: Tomato Pesarapappu
instructions: Pressure-cook moong dal, tomato, chilli and turmeric until soft.
Whisk lightly and adjust with hot water.
Temper mustard seeds, cumin and curry leaves in one teaspoon oil.
Pour the tempering over the dal and serve.
substitutions: Use red lentils for a faster stovetop version.
tips: Serve with vegetables and a measured rice portion for a complete meal.

name: Light Bengali Cholar Dal
instructions: Cook chana dal with turmeric until tender but not completely broken down.
Toast coconut flakes in a dry pan and remove.
Temper cumin, bay leaf and ginger in one teaspoon oil.
Add dal, raisins and salt, simmer for 8 minutes and garnish with coconut.
substitutions: Omit raisins if a less sweet dal is preferred.
tips: Keep the dal grains slightly intact for the traditional texture.

name: Shorshe Mach with Steamed Rice
instructions: Soak mustard seeds for 20 minutes, then blend with chilli, turmeric and water.
Coat fish with turmeric and salt and sear lightly in mustard oil.
Add mustard paste and enough water for a thin gravy.
Cover and simmer until fish is fully cooked; serve with measured rice portions.
substitutions: Use another firm local fish, adjusting cooking time for thickness.
tips: Do not overblend mustard seeds or the paste can become bitter.

name: Light Sprouted Matki Misal
instructions: Cook sprouted moth beans until tender but not mushy.
Cook onion and tomato with godaa masala using two teaspoons oil.
Add sprouts and water and simmer for 12 minutes.
Top each bowl with a small amount of roasted poha, coriander and lemon instead of fried farsan.
substitutions: Use sprouted moong if moth beans are unavailable.
tips: Serve extra chilli separately so spice can be adjusted.

name: Zunka with Jowar Bhakri
instructions: Make soft jowar dough with hot water, pat into bhakri and cook on a hot tawa.
Temper mustard seeds, then sauté onion and chilli.
Whisk chickpea flour with water and salt, pour into the pan and stir continuously.
Cook until crumbly and aromatic; finish with coriander and serve with bhakri.
substitutions: Serve zunka with brown rice if making bhakri is difficult.
tips: Add the chickpea slurry gradually to avoid lumps.

name: Mushroom Xacuti
instructions: Dry-roast coconut, coriander seeds, chilli and poppy seeds, then blend with water.
Sauté onion in two teaspoons oil until golden.
Add mushrooms and cook until they release moisture.
Stir in xacuti paste and water, then simmer for 12 minutes.
substitutions: Use cauliflower florets instead of mushrooms.
tips: Roast spices on low heat to avoid bitterness.

name: Goan Prawn Curry
instructions: Blend tomato, coriander seeds and chilli into a smooth paste.
Cook the paste in one teaspoon oil until aromatic.
Add light coconut milk and kokum and simmer gently for 6 minutes.
Add prawns and cook only until opaque and fully cooked, about 4 to 6 minutes.
substitutions: Use firm white fish if shellfish is suitable to replace but fish is acceptable.
tips: Overcooking makes prawns rubbery; remove from heat as soon as they are opaque.

name: Kashmiri Rajma Gogji
instructions: Heat mustard oil, add turnip and cook until lightly coloured.
Add fennel, dry ginger and chilli powder with a splash of water.
Add kidney beans and two cups water.
Simmer until turnip is tender and the gravy lightly thickens.
substitutions: Use a neutral oil if mustard is unsuitable.
tips: Boil dried kidney beans thoroughly before adding them to the curry.

name: Assamese Masor Tenga
instructions: Rub fish with turmeric and salt and sear lightly in one teaspoon oil.
Temper mustard seeds, add tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add water and simmer for 8 minutes, then add fish.
Cook until fish is done and finish off heat with lemon and coriander.
substitutions: Use elephant apple instead of lemon when available.
tips: Add the souring ingredient gradually and taste.

name: Black Rice Tofu Vegetable Bowl
instructions: Press and cube tofu, then pan-sear in one teaspoon oil until golden.
Stir-fry greens briefly with ginger and salt.
Divide black rice, greens and tofu among bowls.
Finish with sesame seeds, lime and fresh chilli if desired.
substitutions: Use cooked chickpeas instead of tofu for a soy-free bowl.
tips: Use local seasonal greens such as lai xaak when available.

name: Soya Keema Vegetable Biryani
instructions: Squeeze excess water from rehydrated soya granules.
Cook soya and vegetables with biryani masala using two teaspoons oil.
Parboil rice separately, then layer it over the mixture with mint and onion.
Cover tightly and cook on very low heat for 15 minutes before resting for 10 minutes.
substitutions: Use cooked brown lentils instead of soya granules.
tips: Measure oil and fried onion to keep calories controlled.

name: Egg Bhurji with Whole-Wheat Roti
instructions: Sauté onion and capsicum in two teaspoons oil until just soft.
Add tomato, turmeric, chilli and salt and cook until moisture reduces.
Add beaten eggs and stir on medium-low heat until fully cooked but still soft.
Finish with coriander and serve two small rotis per person.
substitutions: Serve with jowar roti for a gluten-free meal.
tips: Cook eggs to 71°C or until no liquid egg remains.

name: Chicken Tikka Quinoa Bowl
instructions: Marinate chicken in curd, tikka masala and salt for at least 20 minutes in the refrigerator.
Grill or bake at 210°C until the thickest pieces reach 74°C.
Grill capsicum alongside the chicken.
Serve chicken and vegetables over measured quinoa with cucumber and lemon.
substitutions: Use unsweetened plant curd in the marinade for dairy-free preparation.
tips: Do not reuse raw-chicken marinade unless it is boiled thoroughly.

name: Jain Lauki Chana Dal
instructions: Pressure-cook chana dal and bottle gourd with turmeric until tender.
Temper cumin and asafoetida in one teaspoon oil.
Add tomato and spices and cook until soft.
Mix in cooked dal and gourd, simmer for 8 minutes and finish with lemon.
substitutions: Omit ginger for stricter household Jain practice.
Use a certified gluten-free asafoetida or omit it.
tips: This version contains no onion, garlic or root vegetables other than optional ginger.

name: Jain Palak Corn
instructions: Blanch spinach briefly, cool and blend with green chilli.
Cook tomato and cumin in one teaspoon oil until soft.
Add spinach puree and corn and simmer for 8 minutes.
Lower the heat, stir in whisked curd and warm gently without boiling.
substitutions: Use unsweetened plant curd for a vegan version.
tips: This version avoids onion, garlic and root vegetables.

name: High-Protein Sprouts Bhel
instructions: Cool steamed sprouts completely.
Combine sprouts, roasted chana, tomato, onion, cucumber and chutney.
Add puffed rice immediately before serving.
Season with lemon, roasted cumin and chilli to taste.
substitutions: Omit onion for a no-onion version.
Use unsweetened tamarind water instead of sweet chutney.
tips: Use safely handled sprouts and steam them before serving to higher-risk people.

name: Low-Oil Pav Bhaji
instructions: Steam cauliflower, peas and potato until tender.
Cook capsicum and tomato with pav bhaji masala using two teaspoons oil.
Add steamed vegetables and mash to the preferred texture, loosening with water.
Toast pav on a dry pan and serve two small pav with bhaji per person.
substitutions: Serve with gluten-free millet roti instead of pav.
tips: Cauliflower adds body while keeping the potato portion moderate.

name: Light Kerala Chicken Ishtu
instructions: Simmer chicken, ginger, pepper and a little water until the chicken begins to cook.
Add carrot and beans and cook until tender.
Pour in light coconut milk and simmer gently until chicken reaches 74°C.
Season with salt and curry leaves.
substitutions: Use firm tofu for a vegan adaptation, noting the soy allergen.
tips: Keep raw chicken separate from ready-to-eat ingredients.

name: Bengali Vegetable Moong Khichuri
instructions: Dry-roast moong dal until aromatic and rinse with rice.
Add vegetables, ginger, turmeric, salt and four cups water.
Cook covered until soft and porridge-like, adding water as needed.
Finish with measured ghee and garam masala.
substitutions: Use one teaspoon mustard oil instead of ghee for a vegan version.
tips: Keep the texture loose; khichuri thickens as it cools.

name: Bajra Moong Dal Khichdi
instructions: Drain soaked millet and combine with rinsed moong dal, turmeric and four cups water.
Pressure-cook until soft.
Stir in peas and simmer until cooked, adding hot water if needed.
Temper cumin in one teaspoon oil, add to the khichdi and finish with lemon.
substitutions: Use foxtail millet when pearl millet is unavailable.
tips: Soaking time is additional and helps the millet cook evenly.
